heroOverview: Heartburn is a substernal burning sensation caused by gastric acid and sometimes bile refluxing from the stomach into the esophagus, activating acid-sensitive nociceptors in the esophageal mucosa. It is one of the most prevalent gastrointestinal symptoms worldwide: population surveys from the United States and Europe estimate that roughly 20-28% of adults experience heartburn at least weekly. Despite its name, heartburn has no cardiac origin — the discomfort arises from the esophagus, which runs directly behind the sternum. When heartburn occurs at least twice a week and reduces quality of life, it meets criteria for gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD), but the symptom itself is the presenting concern for millions who seek over-the-counter antacids before reaching a clinician. Most episodes are controlled with lifestyle modification and acid-suppressive medication; a small proportion require endoscopic or surgical intervention.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 20-28% of Western adults experience heartburn at least weekly; up to 40-44% report monthly episodes (ACG 2022 guidelines)
keyStats.demographics: Affects men and women roughly equally; modestly more common in women during pregnancy; higher rates in people with obesity and hiatal hernia
keyStats.avgAge: Can occur at any age but most prevalent in adults aged 40-60; incidence rises with age and BMI
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 390-400 million people affected by GERD symptoms (including heartburn) worldwide

definition: Heartburn (ICD-10: R12) is the subjective experience of a burning, rising discomfort felt behind the sternum, typically extending from the epigastrium toward the throat. It is produced when acidic gastric contents — or, in some patients, alkaline bile — reflux past an incompetent or transiently relaxed lower esophageal sphincter (LES) and contact the esophageal mucosa. The esophagus lacks the mucus and bicarbonate protection of the stomach, so acid causes direct mucosal irritation, activating transient receptor potential (TRPV1) channels and causing the characteristic burning quality. Heartburn is categorized under General Medicine and gastroenterology. As a symptom it may be isolated (non-erosive reflux disease, NERD) or accompany visible mucosal damage (erosive esophagitis, grades A-D of the Los Angeles classification), Barrett's esophagus, or peptic stricture. The American College of Gastroenterology (ACG) and European GERD guidelines define clinically significant heartburn as occurring two or more days per week with meaningful impact on quality of life. Heartburn can also be functional — meeting symptom criteria without objective evidence of abnormal acid exposure on pH testing, the so-called functional heartburn category in Rome IV, which is thought to reflect esophageal hypersensitivity.

typesClassification.description: Typical heartburn symptoms with normal-appearing mucosa on endoscopy. Esophageal pH monitoring confirms abnormal acid exposure. The most common form of GERD-related heartburn, accounting for roughly 60-70% of cases.

Heartburn with visible esophageal mucosal damage — redness, erosions, or ulcers — graded A-D on the Los Angeles scale. Grade C-D (extensive confluent erosions) carries risk of stricture and Barrett's esophagus.

Heartburn symptoms without abnormal acid exposure on pH monitoring and without motility disorder, classified under Rome IV. Likely reflects esophageal hypersensitivity or central sensitization rather than excess acid.
Affects up to 72% of pregnant women, driven by progesterone relaxation of the LES, upward displacement of the stomach by the growing uterus, and delayed gastric emptying. Usually resolves postpartum.

primarySymptoms: A burning sensation rising from the upper abdomen or lower chest toward the throat, typically occurring after meals, when bending over, or when lying down.
Acid regurgitation — a sour or bitter fluid rising into the back of the throat or mouth, often accompanying the burning feeling.
Chest discomfort that is worse after large, fatty, or spicy meals, alcohol, or coffee, and typically eases with antacids within minutes.
Worsening symptoms when lying flat at night, causing sleep disturbance or waking the person from sleep with a burning sensation.
A sour taste in the mouth or sensation of fluid at the back of the throat, particularly upon waking.
Increased belching or a feeling of a lump in the throat (globus) accompanying the heartburn.
Worsening discomfort when bending forward or stooping, due to increased intra-abdominal pressure pushing stomach contents upward.
Relief within 15-30 minutes of taking an antacid, which is diagnostically helpful in distinguishing heartburn from cardiac chest pain.
Upper abdominal discomfort or bloating following eating, particularly after high-fat or large meals.
Occasional mild nausea, especially with morning heartburn or in pregnancy.
earlyWarningSigns: Occasional mild burning behind the sternum after spicy or fatty meals that resolves spontaneously.
Noticing an acid taste after bending to pick something up.
Mild throat clearing or mild hoarseness on waking that resolves within an hour.
Heartburn occurring once or twice a week that has not yet disrupted sleep.
emergencySigns: Severe, crushing, or pressure-type chest pain radiating to the jaw, left arm, or back — must be assessed immediately to exclude myocardial infarction.
Difficulty swallowing (dysphagia) alongside heartburn, which may indicate a peptic stricture or esophageal cancer requiring urgent endoscopy.
Vomiting blood or passing black tarry stools, indicating upper GI bleeding — a medical emergency.
Heartburn accompanied by unexplained weight loss, which should prompt urgent investigation for esophageal or gastric malignancy.
Severe heartburn in late pregnancy accompanied by headache, visual changes, or right upper quadrant pain, which may indicate pre-eclampsia.

causes.description: The most common mechanism in GERD and heartburn. TLESRs are reflex relaxations of the LES unrelated to swallowing, triggered by gastric distension after meals; they allow gastric acid to escape into the esophagus and account for most heartburn episodes even in people without hiatal hernia.

When part of the stomach slides through the diaphragmatic hiatus into the chest, the LES is displaced from the pressure support of the diaphragm. This reduces the barrier function against reflux, increases acid exposure time, and impairs esophageal acid clearance, producing more severe and persistent heartburn.

Excess visceral fat elevates intra-abdominal pressure, pushes the stomach upward, widens the hiatus, and promotes TLESRs. Obesity increases the risk of GERD, erosive esophagitis, and Barrett's esophagus in a dose-dependent relationship with BMI.

High-fat foods, alcohol, caffeine, chocolate, peppermint, and large meal sizes each reduce LES tone, delay gastric emptying, or increase gastric acid secretion, all of which raise the probability and frequency of acid reflux episodes.

When the stomach empties slowly, gastric contents remain available for reflux for longer periods, increasing acid exposure of the esophagus. Diabetic gastroparesis, post-surgical changes, and idiopathic gastroparesis all amplify heartburn through this mechanism.

Calcium channel blockers, nitrates, anticholinergics, beta-agonists, theophylline, bisphosphonates, iron supplements, and NSAIDs all either relax the LES, damage the mucosa directly, or slow gastric emptying. Reviewing and adjusting medications is an important but often overlooked step.

riskFactors.description: Obesity is the strongest modifiable risk factor for heartburn and GERD. Each 3.5-unit increase in BMI is associated with an approximately 40% increase in GERD risk. Weight loss of 10-15% reduces heartburn frequency by 40% or more in overweight patients.
Present in up to 50% of people with GERD; sliding hiatal hernias impair the LES anti-reflux barrier and are independently associated with more severe erosive disease and need for higher-dose therapy.
Heartburn affects 40-72% of pregnant women, driven by progesterone-related LES relaxation, increased intra-abdominal pressure, and slowed gastric emptying. It typically resolves after delivery.

riskFactors.factor: Dietary habits — fat, alcohol, coffee, chocolate
riskFactors.description: These dietary items individually reduce LES tone or stimulate acid secretion. Population studies show that diets high in fat and alcohol are associated with two- to threefold increases in heartburn frequency.
Nicotine relaxes the LES and stimulates acid secretion. Current smokers have approximately double the GERD prevalence of non-smokers; smoking cessation improves heartburn control.
Scleroderma causes smooth muscle atrophy of the esophagus, drastically reducing LES pressure and peristalsis. Affected patients have some of the most severe and refractory GERD and heartburn.

riskFactors.factor: Use of LES-lowering or mucosally irritating medications
riskFactors.description: NSAIDs, aspirin, bisphosphonates, and many cardiovascular medications aggravate heartburn. Reviewing and substituting where possible meaningfully reduces symptom burden.

riskFactors.factor: First-degree family history of GERD or Barrett's esophagus
riskFactors.description: Heritability estimates for GERD are approximately 30-40%. Having a first-degree relative with Barrett's esophagus roughly doubles the risk of developing it, independent of body weight.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 40-60 with the highest prevalence, though all age groups are affected
Pregnant women — up to 72% experience heartburn at some point during pregnancy
People with obesity (BMI ≥ 30) in whom intra-abdominal pressure is elevated
Patients with scleroderma or other connective tissue diseases causing esophageal dysmotility
Older adults, in whom hiatal hernia becomes more prevalent and nocturnal heartburn is more disruptive
Shift workers and people with irregular sleep patterns, who are more prone to nocturnal reflux
diagnosisOverview: Heartburn is typically diagnosed clinically in the first instance. The ACG and NICE guidelines support an empirical trial of a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) in patients with classic heartburn symptoms (burning, acid regurgitation) without alarm features — this PPI test approach has a sensitivity of around 75-80% for identifying GERD-driven heartburn. Investigation is required when there are alarm features (dysphagia, weight loss, vomiting, anemia, hematemesis, age over 55 with new symptoms), when symptoms persist despite adequate PPI therapy, or when complications or Barrett's esophagus screening is needed. Upper gastrointestinal endoscopy is the primary investigation, grading mucosal damage and detecting hiatal hernia, Barrett's esophagus, or malignancy. When endoscopy is normal and symptoms persist, 24-hour ambulatory pH-impedance monitoring characterizes acid exposure time, reflux events, and symptom-reflux correlation — the key test for distinguishing erosive GERD, NERD, functional heartburn, and reflux hypersensitivity. Esophageal manometry is performed before antireflux surgery and when a motility disorder is suspected. Differential diagnosis includes cardiac ischemia (must always be considered when chest pain is present), peptic ulcer disease, functional dyspepsia, esophageal motility disorders, and eosinophilic esophagitis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line clinical test: a 4-8 week course of a standard-dose PPI that produces at least 50% symptom relief strongly suggests acid-driven heartburn and avoids initial endoscopy in uncomplicated cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You take a once-daily PPI tablet before the main meal for 4-8 weeks and report back on symptom change. No special procedure is required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Directly examines the esophagus for erosions, ulcers, Barrett's mucosa, strictures, or hiatal hernia; mandatory when alarm features are present or symptoms persist on PPI therapy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Under throat spray or light sedation, a flexible camera is passed through the mouth into the esophagus and stomach. The procedure takes about 10-15 minutes and findings are available immediately.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures acid and non-acid reflux events and correlates them with symptoms; distinguishes GERD, NERD, functional heartburn, and reflux hypersensitivity — critical for guiding treatment in refractory cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A thin catheter is passed through the nostril into the esophagus and worn for 24 hours. You maintain normal activities and a symptom diary, then return for catheter removal.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures LES pressure and esophageal peristaltic function; required before antireflux surgery and when achalasia or another motility disorder is in the differential.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A pressure-sensing catheter is placed through the nose into the esophagus. You swallow sips of water while pressures are recorded over 20-30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A fluoroscopic X-ray study that visualizes hiatal hernia, strictures, and gross motility; less sensitive than endoscopy for mucosal disease but useful when endoscopy is not available or to evaluate strictures.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You drink a barium-containing liquid while X-ray images are taken. The test takes about 20-30 minutes and involves swallowing different consistencies.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A capsule endoscopically clipped to the esophageal wall that transmits pH data wirelessly for 48-96 hours, providing a longer monitoring window than a catheter-based study.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: During endoscopy, a small capsule is attached to the esophageal wall and automatically detaches after a few days. You wear a small receiver and keep a symptom diary.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is escalated stepwise from lifestyle modification and antacids through to PPI therapy, and in refractory or complicated cases to endoscopic or surgical antireflux procedures. Lifestyle measures — weight loss, head-of-bed elevation, meal timing, dietary adjustments, and smoking cessation — reduce heartburn frequency and severity and enhance the response to medication. For mild-to-moderate heartburn, over-the-counter antacids and H2-receptor antagonists (H2RAs) provide rapid but short-lived relief and are appropriate for on-demand use or mild infrequent symptoms. PPIs are the most effective pharmacological treatment, achieving symptom remission in 70-85% of patients with erosive esophagitis and approximately 60-70% of those with NERD when taken consistently before meals. ACG and NICE guidelines recommend PPIs as first-line for patients who require regular therapy. PPIs are taken once daily 30-60 minutes before the first meal for maximum efficacy; a 4-8 week course resolves erosive esophagitis in most cases, though NERD and functional heartburn often require longer treatment. Twice-daily PPI, adding an H2RA at bedtime, or switching PPI preparations are options for partial responders. Functional heartburn, which does not respond to PPIs, is managed with neuromodulators — low-dose tricyclic antidepressants or selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors — and behavioral interventions. Antireflux surgery (laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication) is offered to patients with confirmed GERD who are intolerant of or inadequately controlled by PPIs, or who have complications such as peptic stricture or large hiatal hernia. Newer endoscopic antireflux procedures including transoral incisionless fundoplication (TIF) are available at specialist centers.

medicalTreatments.name: Proton pump inhibitors (omeprazole, lansoprazole, esomeprazole, pantoprazole)
medicalTreatments.description: The most effective acid-suppressive class; block the final common pathway of gastric acid secretion (H+/K+ ATPase pump) irreversibly. Taken 30-60 minutes before the largest meal. Standard doses are used for 4-8 weeks initially; maintenance therapy is continued at the lowest effective dose in recurrent or erosive disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Healing of erosive esophagitis in 85-90% at 8 weeks; symptom remission in approximately 70-85% of erosive GERD and 60-65% of NERD at standard doses.

medicalTreatments.name: H2-receptor antagonists (famotidine, ranitidine)
medicalTreatments.description: Block histamine-stimulated acid secretion; faster onset than PPIs (30-45 minutes) but lower maximum acid suppression. Useful for on-demand relief and for nocturnal acid breakthrough when added at bedtime to daytime PPI therapy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom relief in approximately 50-60% of mild-to-moderate heartburn; less effective than PPIs for healing erosive esophagitis (50-60% at 8 weeks).

medicalTreatments.name: Antacids (calcium carbonate, magnesium hydroxide, aluminium hydroxide)
medicalTreatments.description: Immediately neutralize existing acid in the esophagus and stomach, providing relief within 5-15 minutes. Appropriate for mild, infrequent episodes; not suitable as sole therapy for frequent or erosive heartburn due to short duration of action (30-60 minutes).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Immediate symptom relief in approximately 70-80% of mild single episodes; no evidence of mucosal healing.

medicalTreatments.name: Alginates (e.g. Gaviscon)
medicalTreatments.description: Form a gel raft on top of gastric contents that mechanically prevents acid from refluxing into the esophagus. Particularly effective for postprandial and nocturnal heartburn; often combined with antacids for additive effect.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom reduction roughly comparable to antacids in mild GERD; clinical trials show 65-70% response for meal-related heartburn, with a faster onset than PPIs.

medicalTreatments.name: Neuromodulators (low-dose tricyclics, SSRIs)
medicalTreatments.description: Amitriptyline 10-25 mg at night or citalopram 20 mg daily are used for functional heartburn and reflux hypersensitivity that does not respond to PPIs. They reduce esophageal sensitivity rather than acid secretion.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 40-60% reduction in heartburn frequency and severity in functional heartburn; PPI-refractory cases show 50% or better response in randomized trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Baclofen
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces transient LES relaxations and supragastric reflux episodes; used as add-on therapy for refractory GERD at 10-20 mg three times daily. Limited by central nervous system side effects including sedation, dizziness, and cognitive effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces reflux episodes by approximately 40-50% as add-on therapy in GERD refractory to PPIs.

surgicalOptions.name: Laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication
surgicalOptions.description: The gastric fundus is wrapped 360° around the lower esophagus laparoscopically, mechanically reinforcing the anti-reflux barrier. The most established antireflux procedure; performed under general anesthesia with a 1-2 night hospital stay.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Approximately 85-90% of patients achieve significant heartburn relief at 5 years; 10-15% develop dysphagia or bloating post-operatively; long-term recurrence rate roughly 10-20% at 10 years.

surgicalOptions.name: Transoral incisionless fundoplication (TIF)
surgicalOptions.description: An endoscopic procedure using a device passed through the mouth to create a valve at the gastroesophageal junction without skin incisions; suitable for patients with smaller hiatal hernias (<2 cm) and mild-moderate disease.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 60-70% of patients achieve PPI freedom at 5 years in prospective studies; less durable than surgical fundoplication but with fewer side effects.

alternativeTreatments.name: Head-of-bed elevation
alternativeTreatments.description: Raising the head of the bed by 15-20 cm (6-8 inches) using bed risers or a wedge pillow significantly reduces nocturnal acid exposure time by using gravity to keep gastric contents below the LES. Clinically validated and recommended by all major guidelines as first-line for nocturnal heartburn.

alternativeTreatments.name: Weight loss
alternativeTreatments.description: For overweight patients (BMI ≥ 25), every 3.5-unit BMI reduction is associated with approximately 40% reduction in GERD risk. Combined behavioral and dietary weight loss programs achieve clinically meaningful heartburn improvement alongside the metabolic benefits.

alternativeTreatments.name: Hypnotherapy and gut-directed hypnosis
alternativeTreatments.description: Gut-directed hypnotherapy has evidence in functional GI conditions including functional heartburn; it targets central sensitization and esophageal hypersensitivity and may complement pharmacological treatment in refractory cases.

whySeeSpecialist: A gastroenterologist should be involved when heartburn has not responded to 8 weeks of standard PPI therapy, when alarm symptoms develop at any time (dysphagia, weight loss, hematemesis, anemia), when heartburn recurs repeatedly off medication, or when antireflux surgery or endoscopic procedure is being considered. Patients with longstanding weekly heartburn over many years, a family history of Barrett's esophagus, or who smoke and are male have higher Barrett's risk and should discuss endoscopic screening with their clinician.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a gastroenterologist with access to high-resolution esophageal manometry and pH-impedance monitoring, as these are essential for evaluating PPI-refractory heartburn accurately. For surgical referral, seek a center with high-volume laparoscopic antireflux surgery practice. Ask about the clinician's approach to functional heartburn and whether they offer behavioral or psychological treatment pathways alongside medication.
preventionStrategies: Maintain a BMI under 25 — weight loss is the most durable modifier of heartburn risk.
Stop smoking, which doubles GERD prevalence and impairs esophageal acid clearance.
Eat smaller meals and avoid eating within 2-3 hours of going to bed.
Elevate the head of the bed for nocturnal heartburn.
Limit dietary triggers — high-fat meals, alcohol, caffeine, chocolate, carbonated drinks, and large meals.
Review medications that lower LES pressure with your clinician and substitute where possible.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid lying down or bending for at least two to three hours after meals.
Wear loose-fitting clothing around the waist to reduce intra-abdominal pressure.
Eat slowly and chew thoroughly; large boluses distend the stomach and trigger TLESRs.
Keep a food diary to identify personal triggers and eliminate those that consistently cause symptoms.
Elevate the head of the bed by 15-20 cm with bed risers or a wedge pillow for nocturnal symptoms.
Reduce alcohol intake, which relaxes the LES and stimulates acid production.

recommended: Lean proteins — chicken, fish, legumes — that empty from the stomach faster than fatty cuts
Vegetables and non-citrus fruits, which are low acid and high fibre
Still water and non-caffeinated, non-carbonated beverages
Oatmeal and whole grains that absorb stomach acid and support motility
avoid: High-fat foods, fried items, and creamy sauces that delay gastric emptying and relax the LES
Alcohol in any form, particularly wine and beer which are highly acidic and relax the LES
Coffee, strong tea, and energy drinks that stimulate acid secretion
Chocolate, peppermint, and spearmint, which lower LES tone
Citrus fruits and tomato-based products during flares, which directly irritate an inflamed esophagus
Large meals — split into smaller, more frequent portions

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise — brisk walking, swimming, or cycling for 30 minutes most days — supports healthy weight and improves gut motility, both of which reduce heartburn frequency. Vigorous exercise and activities that involve bending forward or inverted positions (certain yoga poses, cycling in an aggressive position) can worsen heartburn by increasing intra-abdominal pressure; schedule exercise at least two hours after meals. High-impact activities during active esophagitis may temporarily worsen symptoms. Weight-bearing exercise to support healthy weight is strongly encouraged as a long-term preventive strategy.
dailyManagement: Take the prescribed PPI 30-60 minutes before the largest meal every day, not only when symptoms appear — consistency maximizes acid suppression.
Keep antacids or alginates accessible for breakthrough symptoms between PPI doses.
Maintain a symptom diary noting foods, portions, meal timing, exercise, and heartburn severity to identify modifiable triggers.
Avoid the 'triangle of risk' before bed: large meal + alcohol + lying flat within 2 hours.
Follow up with your clinician if symptoms worsen, dysphagia develops, or relief is less than 50% after 8 weeks of PPI treatment.
prognosis: Heartburn is a chronic relapsing condition for most patients but is highly manageable with appropriate treatment. Mild intermittent heartburn responds well to lifestyle changes and on-demand antacids or H2RAs; up to 40% of people with mild symptoms achieve adequate control without prescription medication. Erosive esophagitis heals in 85-90% of patients treated with standard PPI courses, but 50-80% relapse within 6 months of stopping medication, meaning many patients require long-term maintenance therapy. Complications — peptic stricture, Barrett's esophagus, esophageal adenocarcinoma — occur in a small minority: Barrett's is present in 5-15% of those with chronic weekly heartburn, and the risk of progression to cancer is approximately 0.3-0.5% per year of Barrett's surveillance. Antireflux surgery provides durable relief in 85-90% of carefully selected patients at 5 years. Functional heartburn, while not associated with mucosal complications, can be challenging to manage and often requires a combined pharmacological and psychological approach.
recoveryTimeline: Antacids and alginates act within 5-30 minutes. H2RAs reduce acid within 30-45 minutes, with maximal effect over 6-12 hours. PPIs require 3-5 days of consistent daily dosing to reach full acid-suppressive effect and 4-8 weeks for complete healing of erosive esophagitis. Lifestyle modifications (weight loss, head-of-bed elevation, dietary changes) can produce noticeable improvement within 2-4 weeks but maximal benefit takes months. After antireflux surgery, most patients notice improvement within days to weeks, with full recovery taking 4-6 weeks.
complications: Erosive esophagitis — mucosal damage from chronic acid exposure, ranging from mild erosions to deep ulcers; heals with PPI therapy but recurs in most patients off treatment.
Peptic esophageal stricture — fibrous narrowing of the esophageal lumen from repetitive acid injury and healing; causes progressive dysphagia and requires endoscopic dilation.

Barrett's esophagus — replacement of squamous esophageal epithelium with intestinal metaplasia in 5-15% of chronic GERD patients; carries a 0.3-0.5% annual risk of progression to esophageal adenocarcinoma.
Esophageal adenocarcinoma — rare but rising in incidence in Western countries, strongly associated with chronic GERD and Barrett's esophagus; surveillance endoscopy aims to detect dysplasia early.
Aspiration pneumonia — nocturnal reflux reaching the pharynx can be aspirated, causing recurrent lower respiratory infections, particularly in older adults or those with esophageal dysmotility.
Laryngopharyngeal reflux and dental erosion — acid reaching the larynx causes hoarseness, chronic cough, and throat clearing; acid in the mouth erodes dental enamel over time.

supportResources.name: American College of Gastroenterology — GERD Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Patient-facing overview of heartburn and GERD from the leading U.S. gastroenterology professional society.

supportResources.name: NHS — Heartburn and acid reflux
supportResources.description: UK guidance on recognizing, treating, and preventing heartburn with clear signposting for when to seek medical advice.

supportResources.name: NIDDK — Acid Reflux (GER & GERD) in Adults
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient guide from the U.S. National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases covering symptoms, diagnosis, and treatment.

sources.title: Katz PO et al. ACG Clinical Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease. American Journal of Gastroenterology, 2022
Gyawali CP et al. Modern diagnosis of GERD: the Lyon Consensus. Gut, 2018
NICE CG184 — Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management, 2014 (updated 2019)
Richter JE, Rubenstein JH. Presentation and Epidemiology of Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease. Gastroenterology, 2018
NIDDK — Acid Reflux (GER & GERD) in Adults
Shaheen NJ et al. ACG Clinical Guideline: Diagnosis, Staging and Management of Barrett's Esophagus. American Journal of Gastroenterology, 2022
Ness-Jensen E et al. Lifestyle Intervention in Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease. Clinical Gastroenterology and Hepatology, 2016

confusedWithConditions.name: Peptic ulcer disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Peptic ulcer pain is typically a gnawing or burning pain in the centre of the upper abdomen that wakes patients at night and is relieved by eating (duodenal) or worsened by eating (gastric); heartburn is a retrosternal burning that rises toward the throat and is linked to position changes and meals, not fasting.

confusedWithConditions.name: Functional dyspepsia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Functional dyspepsia presents with post-meal fullness, early satiety, and epigastric discomfort rather than the rising retrosternal burn of heartburn; endoscopy and pH monitoring are used to distinguish them.

confusedWithConditions.name: Nausea and migraine-related nausea
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Migraine can produce nausea and occasional epigastric discomfort but the cardinal feature is head pain with photophobia and phonophobia; heartburn lacks these neurological features and responds to acid suppression.

relatedConditions.name: Gastroesophageal reflux disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Heartburn is the cardinal symptom of GERD; when it occurs at least twice weekly with quality-of-life impact it meets the clinical threshold for GERD diagnosis and management.

relatedConditions.name: Peptic ulcer disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Peptic ulcers share the acid-excess/mucosal-injury mechanism with erosive esophagitis from GERD; both are treated with acid suppression and both can present with upper GI bleeding.

relatedConditions.name: Gastroparesis
relatedConditions.relevance: Delayed gastric emptying prolongs acid and food contact with the LES, directly amplifying heartburn frequency and severity in affected patients.

relatedConditions.name: Celiac disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Celiac disease causes upper GI inflammation and dysmotility that can produce heartburn-like symptoms; resolution of heartburn with a gluten-free diet in newly diagnosed celiac patients is well described.

faqs.question: What does heartburn feel like?
faqs.answer: Heartburn is a burning sensation rising from the upper abdomen or lower chest toward the throat, usually starting 30-60 minutes after eating. It can feel like heat, pressure, or acid climbing into the chest or throat. It typically worsens when lying flat or bending and is relieved within 15-30 minutes by an antacid.

faqs.question: What causes heartburn?
faqs.answer: Heartburn is caused by stomach acid refluxing into the esophagus when the lower esophageal sphincter relaxes or weakens. The most common triggers are large fatty meals, alcohol, coffee, chocolate, smoking, and lying down after eating. Hiatal hernia and obesity increase the risk by reducing the sphincter's anti-reflux barrier.

faqs.question: How do I stop heartburn quickly?
faqs.answer: Antacids (calcium carbonate, magnesium hydroxide) or alginates (Gaviscon) act within 5-15 minutes by neutralizing stomach acid or forming a protective raft over gastric contents. Sitting or standing upright helps gravity keep acid in the stomach. For more lasting relief, a H2-blocker such as famotidine works within 30-45 minutes.

faqs.question: Is heartburn the same as GERD?
faqs.answer: Heartburn is a symptom; GERD is the chronic disease caused by repeated acid reflux. Occasional heartburn does not equal GERD. Heartburn that occurs at least twice a week, reduces quality of life, and requires regular medication meets the diagnostic threshold for GERD, which may cause mucosal damage over time.

faqs.question: When should I see a doctor about heartburn?
faqs.answer: See a doctor if heartburn occurs more than twice a week, disrupts sleep, is not controlled by over-the-counter antacids, or is accompanied by difficulty swallowing, unexplained weight loss, vomiting blood, or black tarry stools. New heartburn in adults over 55 also warrants assessment. These features require investigation to exclude serious esophageal pathology.

faqs.question: Which foods cause heartburn?
faqs.answer: The most common dietary triggers are high-fat foods, fried items, chocolate, peppermint, alcohol, coffee, strong tea, carbonated drinks, citrus fruits, and tomato-based products. Large meal volumes are a trigger regardless of content. Identifying personal triggers with a food diary and eliminating them reduces heartburn frequency.

faqs.question: Do PPIs cure heartburn?
faqs.answer: PPIs control heartburn very effectively in most people — achieving 70-85% symptom remission in erosive GERD — but they do not cure the underlying structural cause. Most patients relapse within 6 months of stopping a PPI, so ongoing maintenance therapy or lifestyle change is needed to prevent recurrence.

faqs.question: Can heartburn cause chest pain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Esophageal heartburn can produce central chest pain that is indistinguishable from cardiac pain, including pressure, tightness, and radiation to the jaw or arms. Cardiac causes must always be excluded first, particularly in older adults or those with cardiovascular risk factors. Heartburn chest pain typically improves with antacids and worsens with meals or lying down.

faqs.question: Is heartburn dangerous?
faqs.answer: Occasional heartburn is not dangerous. Chronic untreated heartburn from GERD can cause esophageal erosions, strictures, and Barrett's esophagus — a precancerous change present in 5-15% of people with weekly heartburn. The annual risk of Barrett's progressing to cancer is about 0.3-0.5%, which is why endoscopic surveillance matters for long-term sufferers.

faqs.question: What is the best position to sleep in if I have heartburn?
faqs.answer: Sleep on your left side with the head of the bed elevated 15-20 cm (6-8 inches) using bed risers or a wedge pillow. Left-side sleeping reduces acid contact with the lower esophagus. Elevating the head uses gravity to keep stomach contents down. Both measures are supported by clinical evidence and recommended by GERD guidelines.

faqs.question: Can weight loss reduce heartburn?
faqs.answer: Yes. Every 3.5-unit reduction in BMI is associated with approximately 40% lower GERD risk. Studies in overweight patients show that losing 10-15% of body weight produces clinically meaningful reductions in heartburn frequency and can reduce the need for acid-suppressive medication.

faqs.question: Can heartburn cause a sore throat or cough?
faqs.answer: Acid reaching the larynx causes laryngopharyngeal reflux — a pattern producing hoarseness, throat clearing, a persistent cough, and a feeling of mucus in the throat. This extraesophageal reflux is distinct from typical heartburn and often does not respond to standard PPI doses, requiring higher doses or twice-daily therapy.

faqs.question: Is heartburn worse during pregnancy?
faqs.answer: Heartburn affects 40-72% of pregnant women, peaking in the third trimester. Progesterone relaxes the lower esophageal sphincter, and the growing uterus increases abdominal pressure. Antacids and alginates are first-line as they are safe in pregnancy. Ranitidine has been used historically; omeprazole is used when antacids fail and risk-benefit is discussed with the prescriber.

faqs.question: What is Barrett's esophagus and is it related to heartburn?
faqs.answer: Barrett's esophagus is a change in the cells lining the lower esophagus from squamous to intestinal-type cells, caused by years of acid exposure from heartburn. It is present in 5-15% of people with chronic weekly heartburn. It is a precancerous condition that is monitored by endoscopic surveillance because it carries a 0.3-0.5% annual risk of progressing to esophageal cancer.

faqs.question: How long should I take a PPI for heartburn?
faqs.answer: For a first episode of uncomplicated heartburn, a 4-8 week course of a standard-dose PPI is typically prescribed. For recurrent or erosive GERD, longer maintenance at the lowest effective dose is common. Guidelines advise against indefinite full-dose PPI use without reassessment — step-down to on-demand therapy or the lowest effective dose is the goal once symptoms are controlled.

faqs.question: Does milk help heartburn?
faqs.answer: Milk temporarily neutralizes gastric acid and may give brief relief, but the fat and protein it contains subsequently stimulate more acid secretion, often rebounding symptoms within 30-60 minutes. Milk is not recommended as a heartburn remedy. Plain still water or an antacid is a more effective and sustained option for immediate relief.

faqs.question: Can exercise cause heartburn?
faqs.answer: Vigorous exercise, especially high-impact activities like running, and positions that increase intra-abdominal pressure (weight lifting, certain yoga poses) can trigger reflux by temporarily raising abdominal pressure. Exercising on a full stomach is a major risk factor. Moderate exercise 2 hours after eating is preferable, and regular aerobic exercise overall supports healthy weight and reduces long-term GERD risk.

faqs.question: What is the difference between heartburn and a heart attack?
faqs.answer: Both cause central chest discomfort, which is why cardiac causes must always be excluded first. Heartburn is a burning sensation that rises toward the throat, is linked to meals and position, and relieves with antacids. A heart attack causes pressure, tightening, or crushing pain, may radiate to the jaw or arm, and is accompanied by shortness of breath, sweating, or nausea. Seek emergency care immediately if you are unsure.

faqs.question: Can stopping a PPI make heartburn worse?
faqs.answer: Yes. Stopping a PPI abruptly after several months of use causes acid rebound hypersecretion — a temporary increase in stomach acid production above the level before treatment — which can worsen heartburn for 2-4 weeks. Gradually tapering the dose or switching to on-demand H2-blocker use before discontinuing reduces this effect.

faqs.question: Is antireflux surgery better than PPIs for heartburn?
faqs.answer: Laparoscopic Nissen fundoplication achieves long-term heartburn relief in 85-90% of carefully selected patients at 5 years and can eliminate the need for daily medication. However, it carries surgical risks and a 10-15% chance of post-operative dysphagia or bloating. Surgery is best suited to patients who cannot tolerate PPIs, have confirmed reflux on testing, or have complications; it is not superior to PPIs as a first-line option for uncomplicated heartburn.

metaDescription: Heartburn affects 20-28% of adults weekly. Learn its causes, from GERD to hiatal hernia, and evidence-based treatments from antacids to surgery.
